docs.title: What is the handbook?
docs.text: The handbook is a collection of resources for the BHK Lab. It is intended to be a resource for all BHK Lab members to learn about the lab, its projects, and how to get involved.

docs.title: Contributing to the Lab Handbook
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to the Lab Handbook! This section will guide you through the process of setting up your development environment, installing dependencies, and building the documentation.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before you begin, you should have the following:

  1. A GitHub account
  2. A fork of the handbook repository
  3. A local copy of the handbook repository
  4. pixi installed

See Documentation on Setup

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Pixi is a tool for managing conda environments and dependencies. To install Pixi, visit the Pixi website and follow the instructions specific to your operating system. The Pixi documentation is an extensive resource for learning how to use Pixi.

NOTE: Pixi is a new tool and is still in development. If you encounter any pixi related issues when working with the handbook, please open an issue on the handbook repository.

docs.title: Cloning the Repository
docs.text:
To begin, clone the repository to your local machine using the following command:

git clone https://github.com/bhklab/handbook.git

docs.title: Installing Dependencies
docs.text:
Once you have cloned the repository, navigate to the project directory and install the dependencies:

pixi install

This will install the dependencies specified in the pixi.toml file.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: This document will guide you through the process of adding a new page to the handbook.

docs.title: Cloning the repository
docs.text:
Clone the repository to your local machine using the following command:

git clone https://github.com/bhklab/handbook.git

docs.title: Installing dependencies
docs.text:
To install the dependencies for the handbook, run the following command:

pixi install

This will install the dependencies specified in the pixi.toml file.

docs.title: Building the documentation
docs.text:
To build the documentation, run the following command:

pixi run build

This will build the documentation and output it to the site directory.

docs.title: Previewing the documentation
docs.text:
To preview the documentation, run the following command:

pixi run serve

This will start a local server and preview the documentation at http://localhost:8000.

docs.title: Adding Content to the Documentation
docs.text:
The documentation is written in Markdown and can be found in the docs directory.

Here are the steps to add new content to the documentation:

  1. Create a branch for your changes. bash git checkout -b <branch-name> # i.e git checkout -b adding-getting-started-page

  2. Add your new content to the docs directory. bash touch docs/onboarding/getting_started.md

  3. Preview your changes. bash pixi run serve

  4. Commit and push your changes to your branch. bash git add . git commit -m "Add new getting started page" git push origin adding-getting-started-page

  5. Create a pull request to merge your changes into the main branch.

  6. Request a review from a maintainer.

docs.title: Reviewing a Contribution
docs.text:
Note: This doc assumes there is a PR open for review.

Github Docs on Pull Request with Required Reviews

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
After a contribution is submitted and a Pull Request (PR) is created, it must be reviewed before it can be merged into the main branch.

Some terms used in this document:

  • Author: The person who submitted the contribution.
  • Reviewer: The person who is reviewing the contribution.
  • Maintainer: The person who is responsible for merging the contribution.

docs.title: What is a Review?
docs.text:
Reviews are a way to discuss the changes made in a PR. Anyone can review a PR. Reviewers are not required to be maintainers.

Official Github Documentation on PR Reviews

docs.title: What is in a Review?
docs.text:
A review has three possible statuses:

  1. Comment: Submit general feedback without explicitly approving the changes or requesting additional changes.
  2. Approve: Submit feedback and approve merging the changes proposed in the pull request.
  3. Request changes: Submit feedback that must be addressed before the pull request can be merged.

docs.title: Requesting a Pull Request Review
docs.text:
After you create a pull request, you can ask a specific person to review the changes you've proposed. If you're an organization member, you can also request a specific team to review your changes.

Official Github Documentation on Requesting a Pull Request Review

If a doc exists and you want to add to it, look at the bottom of page for any authors.
